coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a highly selective fluorescent and electrochemical sensor for tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
John, Neenamol
Korah, Binila K.
Thara, Chinnu
Abraham, Thomas
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated from R. graveolens leaves following a green and facile hydrothermal process, yielding an 18 % quantum yield (QY). Multiple characterization techniques were used to conduct the optical, chemical, and morphological examination. The surface of the N-CQDs is abundant in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups, which provide excellent solubility, stability and sensing ability to the N-CQDs in the solution medium. The zeta potential analysis indicated that the N-CQD surface is negatively charged (−26.5 mV). It functions as a highly efficient and selective photoluminescent (PL) and an electrochemical sensor for tetracycline (TC). They exhibited excitation-dependent emission behaviour as a PL sensor with an excellent linear range from 0 to 600 nM and a LOD of 60 nM via the inner filter effect (IFE). Excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards TC with a linear range of 0–30 nM and LOD of 0.28 nM exhibited N-CQD as a highly selective electrochemical sensor. Fluorescent paper strips were also designed for the simple and rapid detection of TC present in both solid and solution states. Both developed sensors were effectively exploited to detect TC in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
Inner filter effect
Tetracycline
Paper strip sensor
Electrochemical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD coated paper strips for TC detection
Morphology and structure characterization of N-CQDs
Optimization of experimental conditions for TC detection
Possible fluorescence sensing mechanism for TC
Fabrication of N-CQD coated paper strips for TC detection
Electrochemical behaviour of N-CQD/GCE towards the detection of TC
Mechanism of electrochemical sensing via N-CQD/GCE sensor
Sensing of tetracycline in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The University Grants Commission (UGC) of India provided financial support to Bony K John in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) (Grant number BININ01764209).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) HR-TEM (c) SAED pattern and (d) particle size distribution histogram of N-CQDs.
(a) EDX spectrum, (b) FT-IR, (c) Raman analysis, and (d) XRD spectrum of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs, and deconvoluted spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.

Optical behaviour of N-CQDs (a) UV–vis. absorption spectra, inset: photographs of N-CQD solution under daylight (b) The fluorescence emission spectra, inset: photographs of N-CQD solution under UV light (380 nm), and (c) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths.
Impact of (a) pH, (b) ionic strength, (c) storage time, and (d) light irradiation on the PL intensity of N-CQDs.
Fluorescence emission of N-CQDs at various (a) pH and (b) interaction time.

(a) Zeta potential analysis of N-CQDs, Fluorescence response of N-CQDs when (b) adding different drug molecules, (c) at different TC concentration, (d) linear relationship between fluorescence response (F0/F) of N-CQDs and the concentration of TC from 0 to 1000 nM, and (e) photograph of N-CQD solutions containing various drug molecules having 200 nM concentration under UV light irradiation.
(a) Fluorescence response (F/F0) of N-CQDs towards TC in the presence of other molecules, and (b) fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs in the presence and absence of TC.

(a) CV runs of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, and N-CQD/GCE/TC in PBS, (b) effect of pH, (c) effect of scan rate, (d) square root of scan rate vs peak current, (e) DPV of N-CQD/GCE with TC concentrations from 5 to 30 nM in PBS solution, and (f) graph showing concentration of TC vs peak current.
(a) Selectivity study, (b) repeatability, (c) stability, and (d) PEIS of N-CQD/GCE.
Fabrication of N-CQDs for functioning as a dual probe sensor for TC derived from R. graveolens leaves.
Plausible electron transfer mechanism for the sensing of TC on the N-CQD/GCE surface.
Comparison of the LOD value of this work with some previously published works.

Detection of tetracycline in real samples by DPV analysis (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a highly selective fluorescent and electrochemical sensor for tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Catholicate College, Pathanamthitta 689645, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Catholicate College, Pathanamthitta 689645, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated from R. graveolens leaves following a green and facile hydrothermal process, yielding an 18 % quantum yield (QY). Multiple characterization techniques were used to conduct the optical, chemical, and morphological examination. The surface of the N-CQDs is abundant in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups, which provide excellent solubility, stability and sensing ability to the N-CQDs in the solution medium. The zeta potential analysis indicated that the N-CQD surface is negatively charged (−26.5 mV). It functions as a highly efficient and selective photoluminescent (PL) and an electrochemical sensor for tetracycline (TC). They exhibited excitation-dependent emission behaviour as a PL sensor with an excellent linear range from 0 to 600 nM and a LOD of 60 nM via the inner filter effect (IFE). Excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards TC with a linear range of 0–30 nM and LOD of 0.28 nM exhibited N-CQD as a highly selective electrochemical sensor. Fluorescent paper strips were also designed for the simple and rapid detection of TC present in both solid and solution states. Both developed sensors were effectively exploited to detect TC in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterial-based research has piqued the curiosity of scientists and engineers all over the world over the last few years [1]. Carbon Dots (CDs) are a new class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials discovered fortuitouslyin 2004 by Xu et al. while decontaminating single-walled carbon nanotubes [2]. Later, in 2006, Sun and his group synthesized stable photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles of various sizes and termed them ‘carbon quantum dots’ (CQDs) [3]. After that, within a year, Sun et al. reported water-soluble CQDs passivated with poly-propionylethylenimine-co-ethylenimine [4]. They have a spherical shape with less than 10 nm average diameter. Both sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon networks make up their structure. Furthermore, their surfaces can be functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino, and epoxy groups, offering additional benefits for organic and inorganic moiety binding. The CQDs also have strong photoluminescent signals due to their surface modifications. The surface alteration of CQD reflects a subtle change in their characteristics [5].

Synthetic pathways for CQDs are primarily classified into two categories: (a) bottom-up and (b) top-down methods. CQDs are made from small molecules in the bottom-up approach using microwave irradiation [6], hydrothermal treatment [7,8], or pyrolysis [9,10]. Laser ablation [3], electrochemical oxidation [11,12], and the arc discharge method [2,13] are all used in the top-down strategy to generate CQDs. Their excellent photostability, greatly tunable photoluminescence (PL) behaviour, small size, good biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence and extraordinary multi-photon excitation property make them ideal for application in biomedicine, drug delivery, sensors, optronics, biological imaging and catalysis [14-16].

Doping the CQDs with heteroatoms is an interesting and efficient technique to enhance the QY of CQDs. Among various elements, the size similarity between nitrogen and carbon atoms allows effective doping, which could results in the modification of the intrinsic properties of CQDs, including their surface chemical reactivities, electronic properties, optical properties and a better QY [17]. Although N-CQDs with high QY can be derived from small organic molecular precursors, the high cost and toxicity of these chemicals limits their wider applications [18,19]. Hence, nitrogen-containing natural precursors are considered as the cost effective synthesis N-CQDs through green routes.

Tetracycline (TC) is a very effective and commonly used antibiotic frequently used in both veterinary and human medicine. The wide use of this drug arises from its broad spectrum of activity, simple oral absorption, reduced toxicity and low cost [20]. However, irrational abuse of TC in veterinary medicine and food products has resulted in its deposition in daily food, including meat, honey, chicken, and milk which poses a severe problem to human health [21,22]. The impact of exposure to low-level doses of TCs for long periods can cause issues such as the development of drug-resistant micro-organisms, vision problems, allergic symptoms, liver damage and yellowing of teeth in humans [23,24]. Several diverse analytical techniques, such as HPLC [25], capillary electrophoresis [26], electrochemical approaches [27], colourimetry [28], immunoassays and high-performance liquid chromatography [29] are available to determine trace levels of tetracycline in the environment. Howbeit, most of these methods require relatively sophisticated instrumentation, long-term analysis, and trained operators, limiting their practical applications. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop a novel, cost-effective, and efficient approach for detecting TCs.

This paper presents a hydrothermal route to synthesize N-CQDs from R. graveolens leaves (N-CQD) with a quantum yield (QY) of 18 % and its fluorescent and electrochemical sensing ability towards TC. Although carbon-based materials derived from various precursors have been reported for different applications, the direct conversion of R. graveolens leaves aqueous extract to fluorescent N-CQD has yet to be reported. Our work is unique in the following aspects and has several advantages over other works: (i) N-CQDs are synthesized from a commonly available green source, (ii) no sophisticated techniques were used in the development of this sensor, and (iii) the sensor contains no metals, allowing it to be used in a wide range of practical applications without any hazardous or toxic effects. The prepared N-CQD exhibits strong green fluorescence, excellent stability, high quantum yield, narrow size distribution, and easy reproducibility. Subsequently, this work examined the fluorescent and electrochemical behaviour of N-CQDs for sensitive and selective determination of TC in real water samples. The sensing mechanism arises due to the bond formation between the TC and surface groups of N-CQD by receiving the energy from the N-CQD [30]. The working electrode for the electrochemical analysis was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N-CQD (N-CQD/GCE), while the standard and reference electrodes were platinum electrode and calomel electrode, respectively. The sensor performance was maximized by optimizing all of the experimental conditions. Both methods are highly sensitive and selective, in addition to their simplicity, rapidity and wide linear range.

Quinine sulfate (99.0%), 5-flurouracil, vancomycin, doxycycline, tetracycline, amoxicillin, levofloxacin, ephedrine, ciprofloxacin, cephalexin, and kanamycin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. The R. graveolens leaves were plucked from my home garden. All of the chemicals were analytical grade and were used without further purification. Throughout the studies, doubly distilled water was used.

UV–vis. absorption spectra were acquired from a Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV–visible spectrophotometer. FT-IR analysis were performed on a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 400 spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were collected from a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer. The PANalytic X'PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer was used to conduct X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiments. TEM analysis was carried out using JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. The particle size measurements and zeta potential studies were performed on a Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer. A Physical Electronics instrument was used to conduct X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. Raman and atomic force microscopic (AFM) studies were performed on a Witec Alpha300 RA-Confocal Raman microscope with AFM. The electrochemical studies were performed out on a Biologic SP-300 electrochemical workstation.

The N-CQDs were synthesized employing a hydrothermal route with R. graveolens leaves as the carbon precursor. Fresh leaves of R. graveolens were finely chopped, dried and powdered, and 10 g of the fine powder was dispersed in 100 mL of doubly distilled water. The mixture was then transferred into a 250 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h in a hot air oven. After completing the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. Finally, the suspension was subjected to centrifugation at 15000 rpm/min for 10 min to remove the larger particles and further purified through a 0.22 µm dialysis membrane for 2–3 days, followed by freeze-drying. The solid product obtained was re-dispersed in doubly distilled water to prepare the desired concentration (0.25 mg/mL) and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. The procedure for the preparation of N-CQD from R. graveolens leaves, and its sensing applications are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φ = 54%) was selected as the standard, and the QY of N-CQD was evaluated at 380 nm excitation wavelength using a comparative method following the equation,
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
η s
η R
2
Where ΦS is the QY of the probe, ΦR is the quantum yield of standard, μS and μR are the slopes of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot of sample and reference, respectively. ηs and ηR are the refractive indices of sample and reference solutions, respectively. During the analysis, the absorbance was reduced to less than 0.1 to avoid re-absorption by both N-CQD and quinine sulphate.

The detection of TC was carried out in a pH 7 phosphate buffer solution. Samples with N-CQD concentration of 0.25 mg/mL were prepared for the selectivity analysis and sensitivity studies. Different concentrations of TC solutions were prepared and sequentially added to a vial containing N-CQD solution at pH 7 with a final volume of 3 mL. The quenching of PL intensity by tetracycline was evaluated at a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. The sensibility study was conducted by adding different concentrations of tetracycline (0–4500 nM) and plotted the relative change in PL intensity. The investigation about the selectivity of N-CQD for TC over other organic molecules was carried out by adding 600 nM interfering molecules to the N-CQD-TC (200 nM) mixture, and the corresponding changes in PL intensity were plotted after a reaction period of 4 min.

The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the counter electrode was a Pt wire, and the reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode for the electrochemical detection of TC. All the electrochemical investigations were conducted with N-CQD modified GCE electrode (N-CQD/GCE) at pH 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD coated paper strips for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The use of cellulose filter paper dipped in an aqueous N-CQD solution as an inexpensive and straightforward quantitative device for detecting TC. The filter paper was initially immersed in N-CQD solution, taken out after 4 min and dried at room temperature. The N-CQD coated paper strip was then drop-casted with aqueous TC solutions and left to dry at room temperature. A pinch of solid TC was placed on the developed paper strip and removed after 4 min to achieve solid-state TC detection. The difference in fluorescence from the paper strip was examined using a UV lamp at 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images and SAED patterns were used to assess the morphology, particle size and structural order of N-CQD. The TEM images of as-synthesized N-CQDs in Fig. 1 a exhibit the monodispersive and spherically shaped carbon nanoparticles. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1b showed lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the graphitic (002) interspacing due to some amount of crystallinity present in the system. However, the SAED pattern in Fig. 1c demonstrates that the N-CQDs are amorphous in nature, which is in accordance with the XRD pattern in Fig. 2 d. The histogram in Fig. 1d shows the corresponding particle size distribution of N-CQDs from over 60 nanoparticles with diameters of 2–8 nm with an average particle size of about 4.5 nm.

The EDX analysis confirmed the availability of the elements carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), with an elemental composition of about C 50.85 wt%, O 39.06 wt%, and N 10.09 wt% (Fig. 2a). The surface functional groups and structures of N-CQDs were identified using FT-IR analysis. Functional groups withO–H, N–H, C–H, C=O, C=C, C–O, and C–N bands are available on the surface of N-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. The stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds form a broad absorption band in the 3300–3200 cm−1 range [31,32]. A small band at 2900 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds attached to sp3 hybridized carbon atom. The absorption peaks at 1750 and 1580 cm−1 are caused by the C=O and C=C stretching vibrations [33]. A band at 1385 cm−1 is related to N–H bending vibrations, while C-N bending vibrations are associated with a band at 1082 cm−1 [33]. The bending vibration of the C-O bonds is responsible for another band at 1040 cm−1 [34]. The availability of these surface functional groups allows them to have high water solubility without any chemical alteration.

The two broad peaks observed at 1368 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of N-CQDs shown in Fig. 2c correspond to the sp3 hybridized disordered D and sp2 hybridized graphitic G bands, respectively [35]. The ID and IG symbols indicate the peak intensities of the D band and G band, respectively. The greater intensity of the G band in comparison to the D band is attributable to the less faulty carbon structures in the synthesized N-CQDs. The calculated ID/IG intensity was 0.86, which is less than one, indicating that the carbon components were well graphitized [36]. Therefore, the Raman result suggests that developed N-CQDs are primarily made of sp2 hybrid carbons with some defective sp3 hybrid carbons with a graphitic structure. The presence of abundant sp2 hybrid carbons along with some defective sp3 hybrid carbons was confirmed from this analysis. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2d) of N-CQDs displayed a single broad peak at 2θ = 24.05°, corresponding to (002) graphitic lattice plane, revealing the amorphous character of surface structure and the disordered structure of carbon framework [37].

XPS analysis was performed to examine the surface compositions of the developed N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, three peaks in the XPS survey spectrum correspond to the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s at 284.70, 400.18 and 531.75 eV, respectively. Upon high-resolution analysis, the C 1 s band can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.7 and 288.9 eV indicating C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O, and C=N/C=O moieties, respectively (Fig. 3b). The N 1S spectrum comprises two peaks at 399.23 and 400.93 eV, demonstrating C-N and N–H species, respectively (Fig. 3c). In the O 1 s deconvoluted spectrum (Fig. 3d), three peaks were observed at 534.01 532.91, and 531.62, indicating C–OH/C–O–C, C-O and C=O/C=N, respectively [38-40]. The XPS findings are in good agreement with the FT-IR results.

The size of the developed quantum dots was further investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results (Fig. S1a and S1b) demonstrate that the particle size is well below 10 nm, with an average value of 4.8 nm, close to the TEM analysis results.

The optical characteristics of N-CQDs were investigated using absorption and fluorescence spectral studies. The UV–vis. spectrum exhibits two peaks at 210 and 279 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C and n-π* transitions of the C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4b depicts the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The aqueous N-CQD solution is brown under visible light (inset in Fig. 4a) and exhibits green luminescence at 380 nm excitation (inset in Fig. 4b). The strong fluorescence resulted from the formation of excited defect surface states in N-CQDs [41]. The PL spectra of synthesized N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are depicted in Fig. 4c, demonstrating excitation-dependent emission behaviour. The intensity of the emission peak initially increases with increasing the excitation wavelengths and reaches maximum intensity at 380 nm. A gradual decrease in emission intensity was observed on further increasing the excitation wavelength. This behaviour resulted from the particle size difference of the N-CQDs and the distribution of various surface states due to the various surface organic groups on N-CQDs. The QY was 18 % determined using Eq. (1) with quinine sulphate as the reference sample (QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 380 nm).

To examine the stability of the developed N-CQDs, the effects of ionic strength, pH, photo irradiation and storage time on the PL intensity were examined [42]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PL emission intensity remains almost stable between the pH range 3 to 12, then decreases at higher pH. The PL intensity was almost unchanged by varying the ionic strength of the solution from zero to two molar KCl solutions. This is due to the exceptional salt-resistant behaviour of N-CQDs, as given in Fig. 5b. Compared to newly synthesized N-CQDs, a sample stored at 4 °c for three months showed just a tiny change in PL intensity (Fig. 5c). The PL intensity remains unchanged during the continuous irradiation of visible light for 2 h, demonstrating the high photostability of the system (Fig.. 5d). These findings reveal that N-CQDs have improved stability, making them suitable as PL probe for a wide range of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve excellent sensitivity throughout the sensing process, experimental parameters such as pH and interaction time were investigated. The influence of pH on the PL quenching efficiency was evaluated in the range of 3–12 using buffer solutions. The PL emission intensity increased with pH, as seen in Fig. 6 a, and reached its maximum at pH 7. Further increasing the pH from 8 to 12 causes a slight decrease in PL intensity due to deprotonation of carboxylic groups and increased repulsion between N-CQD and TC [43]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the interactionperiod of 4 min resulted in the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency. Hence, all further sensing experiments were carried out at pH 7 and 4 min of interaction time.

This study applied N-CQD as a fluorescent probe to detect TC in the aqueous solution under optimum conditions. As described in the experimental section, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was measured by adding various molecules to an aqueous solution of N-CQDs with a final concentration of 200 nM. Since the N-CQD surface is negatively charged (-26.5 mV), as shown by the zeta potential measurement (Fig. 7 a), there must be a strong interaction between the N-CQDs and various groups present on the TC surface, which is responsible for the sensing applications. Hence, adding TC to the N-CQD solution caused a significant quenching in fluorescent intensity compared to others, as shown in Fig. 7b. The sensitivity of N-CQDs towards TC was evaluated by measuring PL intensity with increasing concentration of tetracycline (0–4500 nM) and is shown in Fig. 7c. The steady decrease in the PL intensity with an increase in TC concentration revealed that the fluorescence quenching occurs in a concentration-dependent manner. The Stern-Volmer plot was further employed to examine the quenching efficiency of the sensor towards TC and the the equation for the same is given as,
(2)
F 0
/
F
=
1
+
K s v
Q
Where F0 and F represent the PL intensities of N-CQDs at λex/λem of 330/420 nm in the absence and presence of tetracycline, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of TC. In the concentration range of 0–1000 nM, the Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 7d exhibited an excellent linear correlation with R2 value of 0.995. The linearity of F0/F curve against the concentration of TC, represents their outstanding sensing properties in detecting traces of TC. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the equation,
(3)
LOD
=
3 S / m
Where S is the standard deviation and m is the slope of the linear fit. The LOD of the proposed sensor was determined as 60 nM. A photograph of N-CQD solutions containing various drug molecules having 200 nM concentration during UV light irradiation is given in Fig. 7e, which recognizes the selective fluorescence quenching efficiency of TC. A comparison of the LOD values of some recently published works with the present work is summarised in Table 1 .

The interference studies are essential for understanding the fluorescence probe character and practical applicability of a sensor. The fluorescence quenching response of N-CQDs when 600 nM of other interfering analytes such as 5-flurouracil, vancomycin, doxycycline, amoxicillin, levofloxacin, ephedrine, ciprofloxacin, cephalexin, and kanamycin were added to 200 nM of TC is depicted in Fig. 8 a. The results indicate that even on adding other molecules to the N-CQD/TC system, the specificity and selectivity of the sensor remain unaltered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence sensing mechanism for TC
ce:section.ce:para: The PL based sensing mechanism was investigated by comparing the optical characteristics of developed N-CQDs and TC. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption spectrum of TC exhibited a prominent broad peak at 360 nm that overlapped with the excitation spectra of the as-prepared N-CQD. This finding confirms that fluorescence quenching is caused by energy transfer from N-CQD to the TC, called the inner filter effect (IFE). Since this mechanism reduces the number of photons available for N-CQDs, the quenching effect occurred [47]. Additionally, we performed the fluorescence lifetime experiments of N-CQD in the absence and presence of TC to investigate whether a static or dynamic quenching process is involved in the IFE mechanism (Fig. 8b). The fluorescence lifetime of the N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC is 4.21 ns (χ2 = 1.01) and 4.38 ns (χ2 = 1.05), respectively. Since no apparent change in fluorescence lifetime is observed, the fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to a static quenching effect [34]. Additionally, the reduced PL intensity may be attributed to the strong interactions between TC, and the surface groups of N-CQDs, which transfer photoelectrons from N-CQDs to TC may have provided such a specific fluorescence quenching effect. The electrons in the excited state of N-CQDs would transfer to the empty orbital of the organic groups on the tetracycline surface due to the coordination interaction, resulting in non-radiative electron/hole recombination and fluorescence suppression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD coated paper strips for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the benefit of outstanding responses of the N-CQDs towards TC, filter paper strips encapsulated with N-CQD solution were fabricated to visually sense the presence of TC in the water sample. The N-CQD coated paper strips were prepared following the procedures given in the experimental section. The fabricated paper strips were further immersed in solutions of different analytes for 5 min and allowed to dry at room temperature. All the paper strips displayed green fluorescence under ultraviolet light radiation, while a very weak fluorescence was obtained for the TC coated paper strip (Fig. S3). For the solid-state detection of TC, a little of TC was placed on the N-CQD coated filter paper, and the solid was removed after 5 min. A quenched PL emission was observed when UV light was allowed to fall on the paper strips where the TC were initially placed (Fig. S4). These results demonstrate the exceptional ability of the sensor to detect TC even in the solid state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of N-CQD/GCE towards the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 0.05 μM alumina slurry was used to polish the GCE surface, and about 0.25 mg/mL of the N-CQD solution was dispersed in an aqueous Nafion solution (0.5 %). A 10 μL aliquot of this dispersion was drop-casted on the surface of GCE and dried in air. Further electrochemical analysis was carried out using this modified N-CQD/GC electrode.

The electrochemical oxidation of TC was examined using cyclic voltammetric (CV) technique in PBS containing 15 nM TC solutions with in a scan rate of 50 mV/s in the potential range of 0.70 to 1.4 V. After the pre-treated GCE was modified with N-CQDs, a well-defined anodic peak current was achieved on comparison with the bare GCE (Fig. 9 a). This result suggests that introducing N-CQDs was critical in increasing the electroactive surface area and fast electron transfer in the modified system. As a result, the N-CQD/GCE sensor system can detect traces of TC with absolute accuracy. The CV experiments were carried out over 3 to 7 pH range to optimize the pH for the electrochemical oxidation of TC. The anodic peak was highest at pH 4, while the oxidation peak was lower on increasing the pH (Fig. 9b).

A scan rate study was performed at pH 4 from 10 to 100 mV/s to evaluate the effect of scan rate on the electrochemical oxidation process. The anodic peak currents of TC were enhanced by increasing the scan rate from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s, as displayed in Fig. 9c. A nearly constant value of anodic peak current position and peak potential demonstrates the reversibility of the method. The response is linear with R2 value of 0.996 when the square root of the scan rate versus peak current is plotted (Fig. 9d). This suggests the possibility of a diffusion-controlled electrocatalytic process involved in the sensing process [48].

The effect of TC concentration on the N-CQD/GCE was researched by employing the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique in 0.1 M phosphate buffer at pH 4. The concentrations of TC were raised from 5 to 30 nM during the experiment. As the concentration of the TC increases, there is a gradual increase in peak current, as given in Fig. 9e. Since the addition of a higher concentration of TC causes the shift of peak potential to a higher value, a diffusion-controlled electrochemical process occurred during the analysis. Fig. 9f depicts an excellent linear relationship between TC concentrations and peak current with a linear range from 5 to 30 nM and a calculated LOD of 0.80 nM. These results confirm the outstanding detection ability of the N-CQD/GCE electrode towards TC. This could be due to the faster electron transfer and improved surface area of the GCE during N-CQD modification. The LOD value obtained indicates an exact way of detecting trace amounts of TC in an aqueous solution. The observable linear range considers future industrial uses and assesses the concentration of TC in real water samples. Table 2 illustrates a comparison between the sensing ability of the proposed sensor with the previously published electrochemical sensors.

Further, selectivity studies were carried out using the chronoamperometric technique from 0 to 270 sec to evaluate the behaviour of the developed system to possible interferences. Vancomycin, 5-fluorouracil, doxycycline, amoxicillin, ephedrine, ciprofloxacin, cephalexin, and kanamycin which may interfere with the detection, with a concentration five times higher than the TC concentration (15 nM) were analyzed in the study. The results are given in Fig. 10 a, which shows that a higher current shift was obtained only for TC than for other conventional species, suggesting that the developed sensor is exceptionally selective for TC.

The stability of the modified electrode was tested by recording CV using 15 nM of TC for 70 cycles. Even after 70 cycles, there was no discernible change in peak current, further demonstrating the remarkable stability of the developed electrode (Fig. 10b). The cyclic voltagram recorded for a fresh N-CQD/GCE and the same kept in a desiccator for four weeks produced a well-defined peak with only a negligible decrease in peak current intensity (Fig. 10c). This demonstrates the pronounced reproducibility of the prepared electrode.

Potentio electrochemical impedance spectrum (PEIS) was recorded to characterize the conductivity of the proposed sensor. The PEIS was recorded in a solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl with frequency ranging from 5 Hz to 160 MHz. As shown in Fig. 10d, the proposed N-CQD/GCE exhibits a smaller semicircle, suggesting lower resistance and a faster electron transfer reaction rate, indicating the responsible activity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of electrochemical sensing via N-CQD/GCE sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the electrochemical detection of TC by the N-CQD/GCE involved oxidation of the phenolic group to corresponding ketone present at the tenth position of TC by the action of N-CQDs on the GCE in the 1.3–1.4 V potential range [52]. This oxidation process on the modified electrode increases the anodic peak current. The occurrence of irreversible electro-oxidation on the N-CQD/GCE surface was the reason for the absence of cathodic peak current [53]. The mechanism involved in the electrochemical detection of TC is shown in scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of tetracycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the fabricated sensor for the real sample detection of TC was examined by using tap and river water. The collected water was filtered and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to eliminate the impurities. Fluorescence analysis was carried out after spiking the samples with various concentrations of TC with an incubation period of 4 min. Electrochemical detection was carried out at pH 4 using the DPV technique. Table 3 shows TC recoveries ranged from 98.63 to 100.88 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.18–0.59% in PL analysis. Table 4 illustrates an outstanding detection range from 98.10 % to 101.50 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.75 % to 1.30 % via the DPV method. This investigation has further substantiated the selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed sensor towards TC in aqueous samples.

To conclude, we presented for the first time a hydrothermal method for the synthesis of ultra-small and fluorescent N-CQDs using R. graveolens leaves as the carbon source. The synthesized CQDs have an average particle size of 4.5 nm and a QY of 18%. The N-CQDs were modified to develop a novel solution and a paper strip-based dual sensor for the selective detection of TC molecules. As a fluorescent sensor, they exhibited a good linear range of 0 to 1000 nM with a LOD of 60 nM, which is lower than many reported results. By modifying the GCE surface, the proposed system could be employed as a highly efficient electrochemical sensor for TC with a linear range of 0 to 30 nM and an exceedingly small LOD of 0.28 nM. In both solid and solution states, the N-CQD loaded paper strips were effective in detecting TC rapidly and cost-effectively. Furthermore, a real-sample analysis was carried out employing both PL and DPV approaches, yielding excellent results with a detection of 98.10–101.50%. Thus, the suggested N-CQD based dual sensor for TC could facilitate the development of a simple, sensitive, selective, and quantitative nano-sensing method that is free of any harmful effects.

Bony K. John: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Neenamol John: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Binila K. Korah: Data curation, Investigation. Chinnu Thara: Data Curation, Visualization. Thomas Abraham: Software, Investigation. Beena Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The University Grants Commission (UGC) of India provided financial support to Bony K John in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) (Grant number BININ01764209).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
